title: Unit 29 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά)

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 34: Diminutives and Augmentatives. This is one of my favorite topics because it’s where the Greek language shows its heart. Suffixes that change the size—and the mood—of a word. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why do Greeks love shrinking things so much?

Γεια σας! In Greek, a 'Diminutive' isn't just about something being small. It’s about 'a-ghá-pee'... love. When we add '-a-kee' to a word, we are making it cute, familiar, and friendly. It’s like a verbal hug.
A verbal hug... I love that. Let's start with the king of diminutives: '-a-kee'. It’s Neuter, and it works with almost everything. Athina, give us some 'kawaii' Greek.

'Pai-dée' (child) becomes 'Pai-dá-kee'. 'Spée-ti' (house) becomes 'Spi-tá-kee'. Even 'Ne-ró' (water) becomes 'Ne-rá-kee'. It makes the object sound cozy and approachable. You’ll hear people ask for a 'Ne-rá-kee' at a cafe just to be polite!
It’s very common in social interaction. But we also have feminine diminutives, like '-ee-tsa' and '-oú-la'. How do these feel different?

'-Ee-tsa' and '-oú-la' are even softer. 'Ma-mée-tsa'... mommy. 'Ad-er-f-oú-la'... little sister. They are very common with names too. 'Ma-rée-a' becomes 'Ma-ri-oú-la'. It’s an instant way to show closeness.
Now... let's go the other way. The Augmentatives. These make things HUGE. Sometimes in a good way, sometimes... in a 'too much' way. Athina, what’s the suffix for a giant?

The most common is '-á-ra'. It’s feminine even if the word was masculine! 'Spée-ti' (house) becomes 'Spi-tá-ra'... a huge, grand house. 'Má-ti' (eye) becomes 'Ma-tá-ra'... big, beautiful eyes. If you see a great goal in soccer, we say 'Gko-lá-ra'!
'Gko-lá-ra'! I'll remember that one. There’s also '-on-tas' or '-ar-os' for masculine augmentatives. 'An-dras' (man) becomes 'An-drár-os'... a big, strong man.
Exactly. But be careful. Sometimes augmentatives can sound a bit rough or funny. 'Mý-tee' (nose) becomes 'My-tá-ra'... which might not be a compliment! It’s all about the context.
Pro Tip: Use diminutives freely to sound friendly and warm. Use augmentatives sparingly to express excitement or awe. Athina, do we ever combine them?
Not usually! That would be a 'small giant'... it would confuse the brain. But Greeks use these all the time in nicknames. It’s the soul of our informal conversation. Σας περιμένω να τα δοκιμάσετε!
Excellent work today. You now have the power to resize the Greek world. We’ll see you in Unit 35.
